1.synthesis:
Hannah's song erupts from the act of presenting Samuel at Shiloh. It begins in personal exultation — "My heart rejoices in the Lord; my horn is exalted in the Lord" — but almost immediately transcends the personal to become a meditation on the ways of God with the world. The language is military and royal: the horn of the strong is broken, the stumbling are girded with strength, the mighty have fallen and the weak have risen. The reversal pattern is relentless: the full hire themselves out for bread while the hungry are satisfied; the barren bears seven while the mother of many is desolate. The Lord kills and makes alive; he brings down to Sheol and raises up. He raises the poor from the dust and the needy from the ash heap to make them sit with princes.

The song climaxes in a stunning eschatological assertion in verse 10: "The Lord will judge the ends of the earth; he will give strength to his king and exalt the horn of his anointed." This is remarkable: Hannah sings of the Lord's anointed king when no such king exists in Israel. Saul has not yet come to public life; David is a generation away. Keil and Delitzsch emphasize that this verse is genuinely prophetic — Hannah speaks beyond her circumstances, beyond even Samuel's role, to a royal figure she cannot yet see. The song is thus not merely personal thanksgiving but prophetic proclamation. The Spirit of God who opened her womb speaks through her mouth of the ultimate reversal that will come when the Lord's anointed establishes justice over all the earth.

John Gill notes that the "seven sons" of verse 5 should be read typologically — not literal prediction but the number of completeness, fullness of children contrasted with the barren's previous desolation. Matthew Henry observes that Hannah had exactly five children after Samuel (v. 21), not seven, confirming that the number is figurative. The song's typological and prophetic density is unusual for its setting: a new mother at a regional sanctuary, singing words that the New Testament will recognize as the seed of the Magnificat.
1.historical_context: The song belongs to the tradition of victory hymns sung by women after deliverance: Miriam at the Red Sea (Exod 15:20–21), Deborah after Barak's victory (Judg 5). The specific language of Hannah's song — "horn," "anointed," reversals of fortune — parallels the royal psalms and the eschatological oracles of the prophets. The title mashiach (anointed) in verse 10 occurs here without prior narrative context to anchor it: no king has been proposed, no anointing ceremony has been described. This anticipatory use of mashiach is striking and deliberate, placing the entire Samuel narrative — the rise of Saul, the rejection of Saul, the anointing of David — under the canopy of this song. The song's placement at the very opening of Israel's monarchical history frames that history as the working out of the Lord's sovereign reversals.
1.literary_structure: The song divides into three movements: personal praise (2:1–2), the reversal catalogue (2:3–8), and the eschatological climax (2:9–10). The reversal catalogue is itself a chiastic structure in which each pair of contrasts mirrors the others: full/hungry, barren/mother, dead/alive, Sheol/raised, poor/princes. The final verse breaks the catalogue form and introduces a new element — divine judgment, the king, the anointed — which functions as the theological horizon toward which all the reversals point. Mary's Magnificat (Luke 1:46–55) follows the same tripartite structure: personal praise, reversal catalogue, eschatological fulfillment.
1.theological_application: Calvin reads Hannah's song as a school of theology for the church: in it we learn that God's government of the world operates precisely through reversal — lifting the lowly and bringing down the proud — and that this is not random but purposive. The proud who trust in their own strength become the demonstration that human power without God is vapor; the humble who trust in God are the vessels through which God displays his sufficiency. The song trains the heart to see suffering not as abandonment but as the possible terrain of divine inversion. For those who feel ground down by life's Peninnahs, Hannah's song insists that the narrative is not over, that the Lord who judges the ends of the earth has not forgotten. Adam Clarke observes that the specific reversals Hannah catalogues — hunger, barrenness, death — correspond exactly to the conditions from which Israel repeatedly cried out for deliverance, making the song a compressed theology of the Exodus pattern recurring in every generation.
1.christology: The climactic reference to the Lord's anointed in 2:10 is recognized by virtually all commentators as the first fully explicit messianic prophecy in the historical books. Keil and Delitzsch trace the line directly: this anointed king is first filled by David, but David is himself a type and shadow of the greater Son of David. The reversal pattern of the song — the humble exalted, the mighty brought low — finds its ultimate enactment in the cross and resurrection, where the crucified carpenter is raised to the right hand of Power. Mary's Magnificat, which echoes Hannah's song verse by verse, is uttered by a woman in circumstances structurally identical to Hannah's: she has received an impossible gift of divine origin and sings of its implications for the world's power structures. Luke intends the parallel to be heard: the child Mary carries is the fulfillment of what Hannah glimpsed. The horn of his anointed, exalted in 2:10, is the horn of salvation raised up in David's house that Zechariah celebrates in Luke 1:69.

11.synthesis:
The narrative pivots sharply from the heights of Hannah's song to the corruption in Eli's household. Eli's sons — Hophni and Phinehas — are priests at Shiloh, but the narrator delivers his verdict immediately: they did not know the Lord. Their sins are specific and documented. First, they abuse the sacrificial system: rather than taking the priest's share after the fat has been burned — the prescribed procedure of Lev 7:31–35 — they send servants to plunge a fork into the boiling pot and take whatever comes up, or demand raw meat before the fat is burned, thus taking for themselves the portion that belongs to the Lord. When worshipers resist, the servants threaten violence. Second, they sleep with the women who serve at the entrance to the tent of meeting. The sin against worship and the sin against women are joined as twin expressions of the same abuse of priestly power.

Eli knows about both sins and confronts his sons, but his rebuke is described as ineffective — he "honored his sons more than God." The narrator records Eli's question to them: "If one man sins against another, God can arbitrate; but if a man sins against the Lord, who will intercede for him?" It is a theologically sophisticated question, and completely unavailing. His sons do not listen because the Lord had already determined to put them to death. This divine determination raises a difficult point that Matthew Henry addresses directly: God's foreknowledge of their condemnation does not remove their responsibility; they are condemned because they sinned, not sinning because they are condemned. The hardening is judicial — they have already refused correction through their own wills.

Against this darkness, the narrator interweaves two contrasting notes about Samuel: he was ministering before the Lord as a child (2:11), and he grew in stature and in favor with the Lord and with people (2:26). The contrast is structural: as the old priesthood collapses under corruption, the new prophetic order rises in the person of the consecrated child.
11.historical_context: The law of priestly portions is specified in Lev 7:28–36 and Deut 18:3: the breast and right thigh belonged to the priests after the fat portions were burned on the altar. Hophni and Phinehas violated this by demanding raw meat before the fat was offered — a double violation, taking the Lord's portion for themselves and taking it before God had been served. The women serving at the entrance to the tent of meeting are also mentioned in Exod 38:8 in connection with the construction of the bronze basin; their service appears to be a formal religious role. Eli's situation — old, fat (1 Sam 4:18), sons out of control — reflects a broader pattern of Judges-era institutional decay. The phrase "they did not know the Lord" (2:12) echoes the indictment of Israel in Judg 2:10, placing the sons of Eli within the cycle of apostasy that defines the period.
11.literary_structure: The section alternates between the sins of Eli's sons (2:12–17, 2:22–25) and the faithfulness of Samuel (2:11, 2:18–21, 2:26). This alternating structure is deliberate: the narrator sets the two trajectories side by side to make the contrast unavoidable. The brief notices of Samuel — ministering, wearing the linen ephod, growing in stature — are sparse but luminous against the extended darkness of the sons' corruption.
11.theological_application: John Gill and Matthew Henry both emphasize that Eli's fundamental failure was not ignorance but misplaced affection: he loved his sons more than he feared God. Parental indulgence that withholds necessary correction from children who sin is not love but a form of negligence that harms both child and community. Eli's question to his sons ("Who will intercede for you?") demonstrates that he understood the gravity of sin against God in the sanctuary; his failure was at the level of will, not knowledge. The lesson drawn by the Reformed commentators is that spiritual leadership requires the courage to apply discipline even within one's own household, and that failure to do so corrupts both the family and the community it serves. Samuel's miniature linen ephod, made by his mother who brings it each year, is a tender counterpoint: parental love expressed not in indulgence but in faithful preparation for service.
11.christology: The notice in 2:26 that Samuel "grew in stature and in favor with the Lord and with people" is verbally echoed in Luke 2:52 regarding Jesus. The parallel is not accidental: Luke is drawing the reader's attention to Samuel as a type of Christ — the consecrated child growing in wisdom and stature, ministering faithfully in the sanctuary while the corrupt religious establishment around him decays. Just as Samuel will eventually displace the house of Eli, Christ displaces the entire Levitical priesthood with a better priesthood after the order of Melchizedek (Heb 7:11–17). The collapse of Eli's house through the sins of his sons anticipates the collapse of the Jerusalem temple establishment that Jesus announces and that history confirms in AD 70 — the old order passing away to make room for the new.

27.pericope_label: A Man of God Warns Eli
27.synthesis:
An unnamed man of God comes to Eli with a devastating oracle. The message recounts God's covenant with Aaron and Levi — the choice of Eli's ancestor from all the tribes to serve as priest, to offer on the altar and burn incense and wear the ephod — and then indicts Eli's house for despising the sacrifices prescribed for that service, honoring sons above God, and fattening themselves on the choicest offerings. The judgment follows: Eli's house will be cut off; there will be no old man in his house; his two sons will die on the same day; God will raise up a faithful priest who will do according to what is in God's heart and mind. The sign of the oracle is the death of Hophni and Phinehas together — a verifiable fulfillment that will authenticate everything else.

Keil and Delitzsch observe that the oracle functions on two levels simultaneously. At the immediate level, it announces the termination of Eli's priestly dynasty — a judgment Eli himself receives with notable passivity in the next chapter. At the typological level, the promise of "a faithful priest" points beyond Samuel (who is not technically from Aaron's line as a Kohathite Levite, not Aaronide) toward the ultimate fulfillment in Christ, the great High Priest who always does what is pleasing to the Father. Matthew Henry notes the specific language: the faithful priest will do "all that is in my heart and my mind" — the phrase anticipates the New Testament description of Christ who comes not to do his own will but the will of the one who sent him (John 6:38).

The oracle's rhetorical structure — historical recollection of covenant, specific indictment, announcement of judgment, promise of replacement — follows the prophetic lawsuit form (rib pattern) that will become standard in the classical prophets. This unnamed man of God is in effect the first literary-form prophet of the Samueline narratives, deploying a genre that will reach its fullest expression in Isaiah and Jeremiah.
27.historical_context: The Levitical priesthood's origin in Exod 28 and the specific privileges of Aaron's house are detailed throughout Leviticus and Numbers. The reference to Eli's ancestor bringing Israel out of Egypt (2:27) points to the Aaronide covenant established at Sinai. The promise of "a faithful priest" has been interpreted variously as fulfilled in Samuel (transitional fulfillment), in Zadok replacing Abiathar under Solomon (1 Kgs 2:27–35 — an immediate political fulfillment), and ultimately in Christ (eschatological fulfillment). The mention of everyone surviving in Eli's house coming to beg for a piece of silver and a loaf of bread (2:36) anticipates the reduction of his priestly line to poverty — confirmed when Abiathar is expelled by Solomon and his descendants lose their priestly position. The sign of the two sons dying together (2:34) is fulfilled in 1 Sam 4:11.
27.literary_structure: The oracle follows the classic prophetic form: messenger formula, historical recollection of covenant grace (2:27–28), indictment (2:29), judgment announcement (2:30–34), and restoration promise (2:35–36). The contrast between "those who honor me I will honor" and "those who despise me shall be held in contempt" (2:30) is an aphoristic distillation of the principle that applies to honor in relation to God: one reaps what is sown in one's orientation toward the divine.
27.theological_application: Adam Clarke draws attention to the general principle embedded in verse 30: God's promise that Eli's house would walk before him forever was conditional upon their faithfulness — when they failed, the promise was revoked. This demonstrates that divine promises made to particular human institutions are conditional: they depend on the faithfulness of the human recipients. This is not God breaking his word but God responding to the breaking of covenant by the human party. The unconditional promises of Scripture (Abraham's seed, David's throne) are of a different order from the institutional promises made to Eli's house. For the church, this warns that neither institutional form nor historical tradition guarantees continued divine blessing apart from faithful obedience. John Gill notes that Eli's silence before this oracle — he offers no defense, no negotiation — suggests that his conscience confirmed the justice of the judgment.
27.christology: The promise of a "faithful priest" in 2:35 who will do "all that is in my heart and my mind" and to whom God will build "a sure house" has a trajectory that reaches beyond any figure in the Old Testament. Zadok fulfills it partially; the Hasmonean priesthood violates it; the Herodian priesthood corrupts it further. The Letter to the Hebrews argues explicitly that the Levitical priesthood was always anticipatory of a better priesthood: "The former regulation is set aside because it was weak and useless (for the law made nothing perfect), and a better hope is introduced, by which we draw near to God" (Heb 7:18–19). Christ as the faithful high priest who has entered the true sanctuary, who intercedes perfectly because he always does what pleases the Father, is the ultimate referent of this oracle. The "sure house" built for the faithful priest is the church, which is Christ's body — more durable than any Aaronide dynasty.
